kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Latest OFFICIAL (Tue 07-Jul, Westmetall): $3,119.00 vs 06-Jul $3,105.00 = +$14.00 (+0.45%) — a THIRD straight up-day off the $3,077 (.50) base, priced against renewed Hormuz tanker attacks. Wednesday 08-Jul officials post ~13:20 London. Still ~19% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 07-Jul official $3,127.50 vs 06-Jul $3,113.00 (+$14.50, +0.47%); curve a shallow -$8.50 contango. Three consecutive gains confirm the base at the .50 retracement ($3,077); the next upgrade trigger is $3,260 (.382), with $3,050 the line that re-opens the downside.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 07-Jul official 292,425 t (Westmetall) — a further -3,125 t draw to below 293kt, extending the slide under the ~300kt days-of-cover threshold; ~92% Russian-origin, ~1.2 days of global output. Continuous draws through and past the correction keep the structural-deficit signal intact.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Latest OFFICIAL Tue 07-Jul (Westmetall): $3,119.00 vs 06-Jul $3,105.00 = +$14.00 (+0.45%) — a THIRD consecutive gain, extending the base above the $3,077 (.50 retrace). Wednesday 08-Jul officials post ~13:20 London. ~19% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855). avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 07-Jul official $3,127.50 vs 06-Jul $3,113.00 (+$14.50, +0.47%); intraday the 3M traded $3,103–3,152 and last-traded $3,139 (+0.84%, SMM). Three straight up-days now stand between the $3,077 base and the $3,260 (.382) upgrade trigger — and Tuesday's session absorbed fresh Hormuz tanker attacks. avg = June-2026 mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: 07-Jul official: cash $3,119.00 vs 3M $3,127.50 = -$8.50 = shallow CONTANGO, marginally wider than 06-Jul's -$8.00. No acute squeeze signal — but the stock draw to 292,425 t and renewed Gulf-shipping attacks keep the front end supported; watch for re-tightening if war-risk premia rebuild.

benchmark.name: SHFE front-month (excl VAT)
benchmark.note: Most-traded SHFE aluminium closed the 07-Jul night session at ¥23,055 (+0.50%; high ¥23,130) ≈ $3,009/t excl-VAT at USD/CNY 6.78 (SMM 08-Jul morning briefing). SMM A00 spot firmed to ¥22,940 (07-Jul, +¥100). SHFE sits ~$118 under the LME 3M ($3,127.5) — the import window stays shut while China destocks rapidly (warehouse withdrawals at a four-year high).

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (Rio Tinto & South32 with at least one Japanese buyer, 03-Jul; Bloomberg via Mining.com) — +12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353 and +359% vs Q4-25's $86 (SMM). Renewed Hormuz attacks (07-Jul) reinforce the tightness that drove the settlement. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: SMM Japan MJP spot premium printed $380/t at end-June (+123.5% vs end-2025) — published in SMM's 08-Jul briefing — up from the ~$340 prior public reference and converging toward the $395 Q3 settlement; Hormuz re-escalation skews the next prints higher — verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: SMM Europe P1020A duty-unpaid in-whs Rotterdam $470/t (+64.9% vs end-2025; confirmed in SMM's 08-Jul briefing — no fresh movement vs our last reference). Duty-paid $547.5/t. Underpinned by the 6% duty + CBAM; renewed Gulf risk adds upside — verify Fastmarkets.

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added conversion premium, in-whs dp Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print at run time); conversion premia stay stickier than ingot premia — verify Fastmarkets as post-settlement prints resume.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Freshest public US MW reference: SMM's US Midwest DDP print of 110.5¢/lb ≈ $2,435/t (+18.2% YTD; SMM 08-Jul briefing). Structurally elevated under the 50% Section 232 rate — the tariff-$ component alone ≈ $1,564 at the 07-Jul LME 3M ($3,127.5). Verify Platts MW Transaction assessment.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: Fastmarkets MB-AL-0020 ddp Midwest — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (~$0.20–0.22/lb market premium + 50% tariff; no fresh public MB print at run time; SMM's DDP measure prints higher at ~$2,435 — PRA bases differ). The tariff-$ leg rises mechanically with the LME (now $3,127.5) — verify PRA.

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: DXY edged up to ~100.91 on 07-Jul (+0.03%) and held below 101 Tuesday — rate-hike bets were scaled back after the +57k June payrolls miss, but the renewed Hormuz attacks lifted oil and re-firmed the inflation/rate channel. FOMC June minutes due today (Trading Economics).

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Euro ~1.14 handle: June London close 1.1422 (euro weakened from 1.1683 through June, NBC monthly FX) and the pair is little changed early July with DXY pinned just under 101 — re-validate at the US open.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Offshore yuan ~6.78 (07-Jul, Trading Economics) ahead of China June CPI/PPI Thursday — export competitiveness intact; China's aluminium export ramp (+12–18% seen for 2026, Fastmarkets) continues.

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen weak: ~¥162.5 (June London close 162.53, weakened from 159.15 through June — NBC monthly FX); intervention risk persists — marginal premium support for Japan-bound cargoes.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: Edged up to 4.50% Tuesday — a two-week high — as the Hormuz tanker attacks lifted oil and re-fuelled inflation concern; September hike odds ~58% (from ~56%). CME FedWatch: July hold 73.3%. FOMC June minutes today (Trading Economics / CME via SMM).

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Brent closed $72.36 on 06-Jul (+1.28%) and traded ~$73.3 Tuesday morning after a laden Qatari LNG carrier was struck exiting Hormuz; OPEC+ agreed to pump more, capping the upside — a month ago Brent was $95.6 (Fortune, 07-Jul).

macro.name: WTI crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: WTI opened $68.65 on 07-Jul (Forbes) — firmer with Brent on the Hormuz attacks; the war premium is partially re-pricing after the post-truce unwind.

macro.name: Natural gas ($/MMBtu)
macro.note: US Henry Hub rose to ~$3.28 (week high) on record LNG feedgas (~18.1 bcfd) and softer Lower-48 output; EU TTF jumped +8.2% to €47.97/MWh on 07-Jul after the Qatari LNG-carrier attack — the gas channel is the fastest Hormuz transmission into smelter/refinery costs (Trading Economics).

macro.name: Copper ($/lb)
macro.note: LME 3M $13,360.50/t (07-Jul official, -0.1%) ≈ $6.06/lb — consolidating just under the recent highs; +6.8% YTD, still the resilient leader of the base complex.

macro.name: Zinc ($/t)
macro.note: LME 3M $3,562/t (07-Jul official, -0.6%) — gave back part of Monday's pop; remains the YTD outperformer (+13.8%).

macro.name: Nickel ($/t)
macro.note: LME 3M $16,350 (07-Jul official, +0.6%) — a bounce off 2026 lows; the surplus story is intact (-3.3% YTD).

macro.name: Gold ($/oz)
macro.note: Spot ~$4,127 (07-Jul); gold SLIPPED on the Hormuz attacks — tanker strikes drive energy-inflation/rate-risk (real-yield headwind) rather than safe-haven flows; Comex settled $4,145.3 (-0.24%) (Kitco, 07-Jul).

macro_events: THIRD STRAIGHT UP-DAY: Tuesday's 07-Jul official posted $3,119.00 cash / $3,127.50 3M (+0.45/+0.47%); stock drew a further 3,125 t to 292,425 t (below 293kt). Wednesday 08-Jul officials post ~13:20 London — the count needs $3,260 (.382) to upgrade (Westmetall).

HORMUZ ATTACKS RESUMED (07-Jul): Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat and Saudi VLCC Wedyan struck; third vessel hit within 24h — biggest attack day since the US–Iran deal. US re-imposed Iran oil sanctions; CENTCOM strikes near the Strait. War-risk premia re-bidding (Bloomberg/Al Jazeera/gCaptain).
BASE COMPLEX MIXED on 07-Jul officials: Cu 3M $13,360.50 (-0.1%), Ni $16,350 (+0.6%), Zn $3,562 (-0.6%), Pb $1,891.50 (+0.2%) — aluminium (+0.5%) led, with a geopolitical bid the others lack.

CHINA DESTOCKING ACCELERATES: warehouse withdrawals at a four-year high; billet inventory ~130kt (lowest for the period in ~3 years); A00 ¥22,940 (+¥100); SHFE night close ¥23,055 (+0.50%) ~$118 under LME — import window shut (SMM 08-Jul).

PREMIUM COMPLEX RESET UP: Q3 MJP settled $395/t; SMM end-June prints — MJP spot $380 (+123.5% YTD), EU duty-paid $547.5 / duty-unpaid $470, US MW DDP 110.5¢/lb ≈ $2,435 (+18.2% YTD). Hormuz re-escalation skews Q4 talks higher (SMM/Mining.com).

FREIGHT RISING: BDI 2,797 (06-Jul, +2.94%) after Friday's two-week high 2,717 (weekly +7.6%); Capesize 4,100 (+12% w/w, best since mid-April) — and Gulf war-risk surcharges are re-bidding post-attacks (Baltic Exchange/Reuters).

MACRO WEEK: US 10Y 4.50% (two-week high); Sep hike odds ~58%; DXY <101; FOMC June minutes TODAY; China June CPI/PPI Thursday; US June CPI mid-month; Alcoa Q2 on 16-Jul opens the sector season (Trading Economics/CME FedWatch).
lme_commentary: Wed 08-Jul run (pre-officials) — today's LME officials post ~13:20 London. The latest OFFICIAL close is Tue 07-Jul: $3,119.00 cash / $3,127.50 3M / stock 292,425 t (Westmetall) — a THIRD consecutive up-day (+0.45/+0.47%), with the curve a shallow -$8.50 contango and stock down a further 3,125 t below 293kt. Intraday the 3M traded $3,103–3,152 and last-traded $3,139 (+0.84%), with modest bullish position-building (open interest +103 lots; SMM). Tuesday's gain absorbed a genuine shock: three vessels were struck in/near Hormuz within 24 hours, the US re-imposed Iran oil sanctions and CENTCOM announced strikes — the war-premium channel that had been unwinding since the truce is re-pricing. The read stays a confirmed short-term base with an improving bid: a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count toward a completed correction; first support sits at $3,077 (.50) then $3,050, and a sustained break there re-opens $2,950/$2,894 (.618). China firmed alongside (A00 ¥22,940, +¥100; SHFE night ¥23,055, +0.50%), keeping the import window shut (~$118 under the 3M). Today's officials test whether the recovery extends into the FOMC minutes.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference (early-Jul); LME's Platts-settled alumina contract closed $330 (02-Jul, Alcircle); Q2/H2 oversupply still pressuring; Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 — verify SMM/Fastmarkets

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304 — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference) — verify Platts

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309.4 — CME/TradingView (unchanged print at run time)

alumina_notes: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t, latest public reference) softer on Q2/H2 oversupply vs Platts API region (~$304/t; FOB Australia Platts futures ~$309.4; LME Platts-settled close $330 on 02-Jul); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026 as Indonesia/India refinery capacity ramps into subdued demand — a building ~1 Mt+ surplus.

Gulf alumina-logistics risk is BACK on the board: ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz, and the 07-Jul attacks on a Qatari LNG carrier and Saudi VLCC (plus a third vessel within 24h) mean war-risk surcharges and routing risk must be re-priced, even though transit continues.

Bauxite: Guinea's oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); Metro Mining's record Q2 shipments (1.8 Mt, +7% YoY) add to seaborne length; Guinea's export-curb push (>$100/dmt target) remains the key upside risk.
net_read: BASE HOLDING, RISK PREMIUM RETURNING. Aluminium's Hormuz-driven run to a four-year high ($3,855 cash, 02-Jun) corrected ~19% into early July, based at the $3,077 (.50 retracement) with a C-wave low of $3,061.5 (02-Jul), and has now risen for THREE straight sessions — the 07-Jul OFFICIAL ($3,119.00 cash / $3,127.50 3M, +0.45/+0.47%) printed on the same day missiles hit three vessels in/near Hormuz, the biggest attack day since the US–Iran deal. The physical market remains short units: LME stock drew again to 292,425 t (~92% Russian-origin), Japan's Q3 premium is settled UP at $395/t (SMM spot $380), China is destocking at a four-year-high pace, and EGA runs only ~7% of Al Taweelah pots. The bear case is intact but now carries a discount: EGA is ahead of its restart plan, ~700kt of Gulf metal is framed for H2, China exports run +12–18% and Indonesia ramps — yet every one of those flows depends on a Strait that is being shot at again, and the US has re-imposed Iran oil sanctions with CENTCOM striking targets near the corridor. US premiums stay tariff-inflated (50% Section 232; tariff-$ ≈ $1,564 at LME $3,127.5). Macro is two-sided: DXY under 101 helps, but the 10Y at 4.50% and Sep hike odds ~58% (FOMC minutes today) cap the tailwind, and freight is rising (BDI 2,797). The recovery still needs $3,260 (.382) to become a trend; $3,050 remains the line that re-opens the downside.

feed.text: Wednesday pre-officials check: today's LME officials post ~13:20 London. Latest OFFICIAL is Tue 07-Jul — cash $3,119.00 / 3M $3,127.50 / stock 292,425 t (Westmetall): a THIRD straight up-day, absorbed alongside renewed Hormuz attacks. SMM (08-Jul): SHFE night close ¥23,055 (+0.50%); China destocking at a four-year-high pace; SMM frames a one-sided weak trend as hard to sustain.

Westmetall posted the 07-Jul OFFICIAL: cash $3,119.00 / 3M $3,127.50 / stock 292,425 t — +0.45/+0.47%, the third consecutive gain; stock drew a further 3,125 t below 293kt. Curve -$8.50 contango. Intraday 3M range $3,103–3,152; last $3,139 (+0.84%).

HORMUZ ATTACKS RESUMED: laden Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat struck ~8nm off Limah (engine-room fire, explosion risk) and Saudi VLCC Wedyan hit — biggest attack day since the US–Iran deal; a third vessel struck within 24h. US re-imposed Iran oil-export sanctions; CENTCOM announced strikes near the Strait (Bloomberg/Al Jazeera/gCaptain).

Rates re-priced the attack: US 10Y edged up to 4.50% (two-week high); September hike odds ~58% (from ~56%); DXY held just under 101. Brent closed $72.36 (+1.28%), traded ~$73.3 Tuesday; EU TTF gas +8.2% to €47.97/MWh. FOMC June minutes due Wednesday.

China physical firm: SMM A00 spot ¥22,940 (+¥100); SHFE most-traded night close ¥23,055 (+0.50%). Ingot inventories in major consumption areas fell again (Guangdong/Wuxi-led); billet inventory ~130kt is the lowest for the period in ~3 years (SMM/Alcircle).
Westmetall 06-Jul OFFICIAL: cash $3,105.00 / 3M $3,113.00 / stock 295,550 t (+0.8/+0.8%) — the second straight gain that confirmed the base at the $3,077 (.50) retracement.

Freight extended its rebound: BDI +2.94% to 2,797 after Friday's two-week high of 2,717 (+2.5%; weekly +7.6%) — Capesize-led (4,100, +12% w/w, best since mid-April; avg earnings $33,678/day). Bulk freight for bauxite/alumina is rising again (Baltic Exchange/Reuters).

Q3 MJP settled at $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32 with at least one Japanese buyer) — +12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 consensus; initial offers were $460–480 (Bloomberg via Mining.com). SMM's end-June MJP spot print: $380.

EGA: ~7% of Al Taweelah pots back online, AHEAD of the internal restart timeline; first refinery alumina targeted early Q3; exporting from inventories (The National/Alcircle). The 07-Jul attacks now overlay a re-escalation discount on the H2 Gulf-return framing.

Alcoa agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio — $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~$1.0bn stock; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments), close expected H1-2027; Alcoa becomes the world's #1 bauxite miner (Alcoa PR/Mining Weekly).

news.headline: GULF SHIPPING ATTACKS RESUMED — a laden Qatari LNG carrier (Al Rekayyat, Nakilat) was struck by a missile/drone ~8nm off Limah while exiting Hormuz (engine-room fire, explosion risk) and Saudi VLCC Wedyan was also hit on 07-Jul, the biggest attack day since the US–Iran deal; a third vessel was struck within 24h. The US re-imposed sanctions on Iranian oil exports and CENTCOM announced strikes near the Strait — the H2 Gulf-supply-return assumption is now at risk.

Aluminium logged a THIRD straight up-day: the 07-Jul Westmetall OFFICIAL closed $3,119.00 cash / $3,127.50 3M (+0.45/+0.47%) with LME stock drawn to 292,425 t (below 293kt); intraday the 3M traded up to $3,152 and last-traded $3,139 (+0.84%). The count now needs $3,260 (.382) to confirm a completed correction.

China is destocking FAST: aluminium ingot warehouse withdrawals hit a four-year high last week, billet inventory fell to ~130kt (lowest for the period in ~3 years, -10kt w/w), secondary-alloy ingot stocks are drawing, and the liquid-aluminium ratio keeps rising. SMM A00 spot ¥22,940 (07-Jul, +¥100); SHFE night close ¥23,055 (+0.50%) — SMM sees a one-sided weak trend as 'difficult to sustain'.
news.source: SMM Morning Meeting Summary (08-Jul) — metal.com; Alcircle/SMM billet stats (05-Jul)

news.headline: Q3 MJP SETTLED AT $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32, 03-Jul) — +12% q/q and far above the ~$275–320 consensus; SMM's end-June MJP spot print reached $380 (+123.5% vs end-2025), confirming spot convergence to the settlement. The 07-Jul Hormuz attacks reinforce the tightness premium buyers are paying for.
news.source: Bloomberg via Mining.com (03-Jul); SMM 08-Jul briefing — metal.com

news.headline: EGA remains AHEAD of its internal Al Taweelah restart timeline (~7% of the 1,262 pots back online; first refinery alumina targeted early Q3; exports continuing from inventories) — but the 07-Jul attacks show the ~700kt H2 Gulf-return framing now carries a live re-escalation discount; hot-metal ramp to pre-incident levels still ~a year.
news.source: EGA via The National / Alcircle (02-Jul) — thenationalnews.com

news.headline: Alcoa's acquisition of South32's bauxite, alumina and aluminium assets is confirmed at $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~17.0m Alcoa shares ≈ $1.0bn; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030) — Boddington + Worsley (WA), MRN + Alumar (Brazil) and South Africa ops. Close expected H1-2027; Alcoa becomes the world's largest bauxite miner (equity share 8.5% → 13%), overtaking Rio Tinto.
news.source: Alcoa press release (01-Jul) — news.alcoa.com; Mining Weekly (02-Jul)

news.headline: The US 10Y edged up to 4.50% (a two-week high) as the Hormuz attacks lifted oil and re-fuelled inflation concern — September hike odds rose to ~58% (from ~56%); DXY held just under 101. FOMC June minutes due Wednesday; the post-payrolls dollar relief is fading at the margin.
news.source: Trading Economics / CNBC (07-Jul); CME FedWatch via SMM (08-Jul)

news.headline: Energy re-priced the attack fastest: Brent closed $72.36 (+1.28%) and traded ~$73.3 Tuesday; EU TTF gas jumped +8.2% to €47.97/MWh after the LNG-carrier strike; US Henry Hub hit a one-week high (~$3.28). OPEC+ agreed to pump more, capping crude upside — but gas is the direct smelter/refinery cost channel.

Hydro will close two US aluminium extrusion plants — City of Industry (CA) by early 2027 and Delhi (LA) through Q3-2027, ~350 jobs — citing below-capacity running rates; a reminder that record tariff-inflated US all-in prices are pressuring downstream demand even as upstream stays tight.
news.source: Hydro press release / Aluminium International Today — hydro.com

news.headline: Dry-bulk freight is rising again: the BDI reached 2,797 on 06-Jul (+2.94%) after Friday's two-week high of 2,717 (weekly +7.6%), Capesize-led (index 4,100, +12% w/w, best since mid-April) — bauxite/alumina freight assumptions need refreshing; war-risk surcharges on Gulf lanes are also re-bidding.
news.source: Baltic Exchange / Reuters via Baird Maritime (03-Jul) / Trading Economics (06-Jul)

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio (Boddington, Worsley, MRN, Alumar, South Africa ops) for $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~$1.0bn stock; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 — making Alcoa the world's largest bauxite miner. Q2 results due 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each peer's most recent reported results as of 08-Jul (no new releases since the last run). Q2 season dates: Alcoa 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT), Rio Tinto Q2 production report mid-Jul, Norsk Hydro 22-Jul (07:00 CEST), Maaden (1211) late-Jul/early-Aug. Corporate action: Alcoa–South32 bauxite/alumina/aluminium deal — $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~17.0m shares ≈ $1.0bn), up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030; close expected H1-2027 (announced 01-Jul). Sources: company releases & SEC/exchange filings, Reuters, Bloomberg, BusinessWire, Alcircle.

commercial.text: Three up-days off the .50 retrace ($3,077; 3M now $3,127.5) plus a live Hormuz re-escalation = a firmer bid under the market. Keep LME hedges on priced volume; pace unpriced tonnes into strength — a reclaim of $3,260 (.382) upgrades the count, while $3,050 remains the invalidation line. Do NOT pre-sell the H2 'Gulf-return' surplus narrative: that supply now carries a re-escalation discount.

The premium complex is re-arming: Q3 MJP settled $395/t, SMM MJP spot printed $380 (+123.5% YTD), EU duty-paid $547.5 / duty-unpaid $470, US MW DDP 110.5¢/lb ≈ $2,435 — and the 07-Jul attacks skew Q4 MJP talks (scenario band $350–425) toward the top half. Anchor Q3/Q4 renewals and spot offers to the settlement; capture war-risk pass-through clauses now, before PRAs mark premia higher.

Ex-China FOB Australia ~$340/t (API ~$304; Platts futures ~$309.4) with the surplus capping upside; Nalco guides $320–330 for 2026 and Metro Mining's record Q2 bauxite shipments add raw-material length. If net seller, transact into firmness promptly; if buyer, the surplus caps delivered cost — but freight is rising (BDI 2,797, Capesize +12% w/w) and ~60% of Gulf-region alumina imports transit a Strait under attack again: lock bulk windows and war-risk terms early.

Treat Hormuz as CONTESTED again: three vessels hit in 24h (incl. a laden LNG carrier), US sanctions re-imposed, CENTCOM striking. Re-price war-risk cover and Gulf surcharges immediately; elevate the Jeddah/Red Sea land-bridge from standby to ready; review Incoterms (CIF vs FOB), demurrage and force-majeure language on Gulf-loading contracts. Freight is no longer one-way cheaper — BDI 2,797 and rising.

Use the $395 Q3 MJP settlement, the SMM premium prints and the renewed Hormuz risk to justify firm premiums and secure Q3/Q4 commitments — customers exposed to Middle-East supply chains will pay for certainty of delivery; document the public evidence trail (settlement, attacks, war-risk re-pricing) in negotiations.

Watch, in order: (1) whether attacks continue and how the US–Iran deal holds (sanctions re-imposed; CENTCOM strikes); (2) today's LME officials vs $3,260 (.382); (3) FOMC June minutes (10Y 4.50%, Sep odds ~58%); (4) China June CPI/PPI Thursday and the destocking pace; (5) first post-settlement PRA premium prints; (6) Alcoa Q2 (16-Jul) opening the earnings season.

bottom_line: On 08-Jul the recovery has a new engine: three consecutive official gains (3M $3,127.5; stock 292,425 t and falling) now coincide with renewed attacks on Gulf shipping — a laden Qatari LNG carrier and a Saudi VLCC hit on 07-Jul, a third vessel within 24h, US sanctions on Iranian oil re-imposed and CENTCOM striking near the Strait. The bear case (EGA ahead of plan, ~700kt Gulf return in H2, China exports +12–18%, Indonesia ramp) is intact on paper but every leg of it transits a corridor that is being shot at again, and premia (MJP $395 settled / SMM spot $380; EU DP $547.5; US MW DDP ~$2,435) never priced the peace in the first place. Macro is two-sided — DXY under 101 helps, the 10Y at 4.50% (two-week high, Sep hike odds ~58%) does not. Net: the $3,050–3,130 zone reads as an accepted base; a break of $3,260 (.382) — increasingly plausible if Gulf risk persists — would confirm the correction complete. Today's catalysts: the 08-Jul officials, FOMC June minutes, and any further Hormuz incident; then China CPI/PPI Thursday and Alcoa's Q2 on 16-Jul.

outlook.curve_note: The front end sits in shallow contango (-$8.50 on 07-Jul) — no acute squeeze, but steady stock draws (292,425 t, below 293kt) keep the nearby dates supported, and the 07-Jul Hormuz attacks re-open the possibility of the war-premium/backwardation regime returning if Gulf flows are threatened. Consensus 2026 averages sit in the $3,200–3,500 band before easing into 2027 as Gulf supply repairs; ~700kt of Gulf metal is framed to return in H2 and China exports run +12–18% — but that supply path now carries a live re-escalation discount, and the Q3 MJP settlement at $395/t shows the physical market never stopped paying up. The structural deficit remains the floor under the $3,050–3,130 support zone.

ai_analysis: What changed: Tuesday's 07-Jul officials delivered a THIRD straight up-day (Al 3M $3,127.50, +0.47%; stock 292,425 t, -3,125) — and the session absorbed the biggest Gulf-shipping attack day since the US–Iran deal (3 vessels hit in 24h; US sanctions on Iranian oil re-imposed; CENTCOM strikes). The recovery now has both a technical base AND a re-opening geopolitical bid.

Term structure: cash–3M at -$8.50 — still a shallow contango, but the combination of sub-293kt stock, daily draws and renewed corridor risk is the setup from which June's backwardation emerged; watch the front spreads for the first sign of re-tightening.

Drivers: physical tightness is not easing — Q3 MJP settled $395 (SMM spot $380, +123.5% YTD), EU premia >60% above end-2025, China destocking at a four-year-high pace with the import window shut. Against that: EGA ahead of restart plan, ~700kt H2 Gulf return framed, China exports +12–18%, Indonesia ramping — supply that must transit a Strait under fire again.

Positioning read: SMM notes modest bullish position-building on the LME (OI +103 lots into the +0.84% close) and bear-position reduction on the SHFE — consistent with a market covering the 'peace-dividend' short rather than chasing length; a $3,260 (.382) reclaim is the trigger that would force the chase.

premium_explainer.desc: Quarterly-negotiated premium over LME cash for primary metal delivered to Japan — the Asian benchmark.
Spot premium for cargoes delivered cost-insurance-freight to Japan; leads the next MJP negotiation.
Premium for non-EU-origin metal in-warehouse Rotterdam before the 3% import duty — the European spot gauge.
Value-added premium for extrusion billet (conversion + logistics) on top of the ingot premium.
Delivered-Midwest premium over LME; heavily driven by US Section 232 tariffs and import economics.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 MJP SETTLED at $395/t (+12% q/q; fourth consecutive rise: $86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM's end-June spot print ($380, +123.5% YTD) confirms convergence; the 07-Jul Hormuz attacks skew Q4 talks (scenario band $350–425) toward the top half if Gulf risk persists.
European (EDU): $470/t duty-unpaid / $547.5 duty-paid (SMM) — softer spot demand but the 6% duty + CBAM policy floor holds; renewed Gulf risk adds upside via freight/war-risk pass-through.

US Midwest: SMM MW DDP 110.5¢/lb ≈ $2,435/t (+18.2% YTD); Fastmarkets P1020A last ~$2,045 (PRA bases differ) — structurally elevated on the 50% Section 232 rate; the tariff-$ leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,564 at $3,127.5) rises with the LME recovery.

Common thread: the exchange price corrected ~19% from the June high, but the premium complex says the physical market is still short units — Japan settled UP, EU premia are >60% above end-2025, and the 07-Jul attacks re-injected the war-risk channel premia had only begun to unwind.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM, 08-Jul) while Guinea weighs export curbs toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Fastmarkets/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: ex-China FOB Australia (~$340/t) easing on Q2/H2 oversupply; API region ~$304 (FOB futures ~$309.4). Surplus builds into H2 — but the 07-Jul Hormuz attacks re-introduce a Gulf-logistics risk premium on ~60% of regional alumina import flows.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38) — record Q2 Australian shipments (Metro Mining 1.8 Mt) add length; Guinea's export-curb push (targeting >$100/dmt) is the key upside risk to raw-material cost.

Caustic soda firm (~$500/t FOB NE Asia) — a sticky refining cost; lime stable. Net refining-cost pressure eases with alumina/bauxite, though EU gas +8.2% on the LNG-carrier attack is a reminder that energy remains the swing input.

Read-through: softer alumina + bauxite lower a smelter's input cost; for an alumina seller, oversupply caps realizations into Q3 — and freight/war-risk costs on Gulf-adjacent lanes are rising again, not falling.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)
inputs.src: Peaked ~$896/t (2022); battery-anode demand + tight seaborne supply pressuring — CRU; limited price transparency

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks
inputs.src: Multi-year pot-reline cost; graphite-electrode supply (China) is the risk

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,360.50 (07-Jul official, -0.1%); busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Last print 2,797 (Mon 06-Jul, +2.94%), extending Friday 03-Jul's rally to 2,717 (+2.5%, a two-week high; weekly +7.6%) — Capesize-led (index 4,100, +12% w/w, its best since mid-April; avg earnings $33,678/day). Sequence corrected from our prior note. Tuesday's print not yet publicly posted at run time. Dry-bulk freight for bauxite/alumina is rising, not falling (Baltic Exchange / Reuters via Baird Maritime / Trading Economics).

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.note: 07-Jul: laden Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat struck ~8nm off Limah (engine-room fire, explosion risk) and Saudi VLCC Wedyan hit — the biggest attack day since the US–Iran deal; a third vessel was struck within 24h (IRGC-attributed). US re-imposed Iran oil-export sanctions; CENTCOM announced strikes near the Strait. Transit continues but ~80–85% of Gulf Al exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz — the H2 Gulf-return framing (~700kt) now carries a live re-escalation discount (Bloomberg / Al Jazeera / gCaptain).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: War-risk premia and Gulf surcharges are re-bidding after the 07-Jul attacks reversed weeks of normalisation — keep cover in place; expect quote volatility while the US–Iran deal is tested.

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: Trucking workaround — move from standby to elevated readiness while attacks on Gulf shipping are live; re-validate carrier capacity and costings.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: Re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure and keep demurrage clauses live — routing risk has RETURNED; review force-majeure language on Gulf-loading contracts.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (largest cost — EU gas +8.2% on the 07-Jul LNG-carrier attack is the live risk), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,360.50 07-Jul official).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — candidates to diversify or contract forward.

Logistics has FLIPPED from tailwind to watch-item: Hormuz shipping is under attack again (3 vessels in 24h, 07-Jul) and the BDI is rising (2,797, +2.9%; Capesize +12% w/w) — freight and war-risk assumptions on bauxite/alumina/metal lanes need immediate refresh.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at the $3,855 four-year high (02-Jun-2026) — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave blow-off confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and the ABC correction that followed now looks complete: wave A bottomed at $3,359 (16-Jun), wave B failed at $3,406 (22-Jun), and wave C printed $3,061.5 (02-Jul) inside the $2,950–3,080 confluence (.50 retracement / prior 4th-of-lesser-degree). Three consecutive higher officials ($3,087 → $3,113 → $3,127.5 3M) plus the renewed Hormuz attacks (07-Jul) — a fresh version of the same catalyst that drove wave 5 — mark C as provisionally complete. Base case (60%, up from 55%): the corrective low is in at $3,061.5 and a multi-month recovery leg builds toward $3,400–3,600, provided price holds above $3,050. Bear alternate (25%): the bounce is a lower-degree (b)-wave and one more probe of the .618 at $2,894 completes a deeper C. Bull alternate (15%): Gulf re-escalation accelerates the advance straight through $3,260 (.382) toward a retest of $3,700+. Invalidation: a weekly close below $2,850 voids the corrective count and re-opens $2,600. Confidence: moderate, improving with the three-day higher structure and the returning geopolitical bid.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Daily (swing): from the $3,855 top, wave A fell to $3,359 (16-Jun), wave B was sold at the $3,607 spike (22-Jun), and wave C unwound to $3,065 3M / $3,061.5 cash (02-Jul) — a textbook tag of the .50 retracement ($3,077) of the whole advance. Three consecutive up-days have followed ($3,087 → $3,113 → $3,127.5 officials; last trade $3,139), the longest winning run since the top, with SMM noting bullish momentum expanding on the 4-hour MACD while daily bearish momentum contracts — the base is confirmed and now reinforced by the 07-Jul Hormuz attacks. Roadmap: while price holds above $3,050 the base case (55%) is an extension through the $3,146 (MA10) / $3,180 shelf toward $3,260 (.382), whose clean break confirms the correction complete and opens $3,400; a stall and daily close back below $3,050 re-opens $2,990–3,020 (30%); a decisive break of $2,950 exposes $2,894 (.618) (15%, receding). Invalidation for the base: a daily close below $3,050. Confidence: moderate-to-firm, upgraded on the three-day structure plus the returning war-risk bid.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz — attacks on Gulf shipping RESUMED (07-Jul)
premium_drivers.d: A laden Qatari LNG carrier (Al Rekayyat, Nakilat) and a Saudi VLCC (Wedyan) were struck by projectiles in/near the Strait on 07-Jul — the biggest attack day since the US–Iran deal — and a third vessel was hit within 24 hours. The US re-imposed sanctions on Iranian oil exports and CENTCOM announced strikes near the Strait. War-risk premia are re-bidding and the ~700kt H2 Gulf-metal-return assumption is now at risk.

Q3 MJP settled at $395/t (Rio Tinto/South32), UP from Q2's $350–353 and far above the ~$275–320 consensus; SMM's end-June MJP spot print of $380 (+123.5% YTD) confirms spot converging to the settlement — buyers dependent on Middle-East metal keep paying up despite the LME's ~19% correction.

50% tariff on full customs value (June-2026 proclamation, eff. 8-Jun-2026) → US Midwest premiums remain structurally elevated (SMM MW DDP 110.5¢/lb ≈ $2,435, +18.2% YTD); the tariff-$ component moves mechanically with the LME (≈$1,564 at the 07-Jul 3M of $3,127.5).

EU import duty at 6% (reverted from 4% on 1-Jan-2026) plus CBAM carbon costs → European premiums structurally supported (SMM: duty-paid $547.5, duty-unpaid $470, both up >62% vs end-2025) despite soft spot demand.

so_what.line: Hormuz is back in the price: aluminium's third straight official gain (07-Jul cash $3,119 / 3M $3,127.5, stock below 293kt) landed on the same day missiles hit a Qatari LNG carrier and a Saudi tanker — the biggest Gulf-shipping attack day since the US–Iran deal — putting the ~700kt of returning Gulf metal that the bear case relies on back in question.
points: Exchange: Tuesday's officials rose again — cash $3,119 / 3M $3,127.5 (+0.45/+0.47%), a THIRD consecutive up-day, with LME stock drawn another 3,125 t to 292,425 t. The base at $3,077 (.50) is holding; $3,260 (.382) is the level that would upgrade the recovery to a completed correction, $3,050 the line that re-opens the downside.

Geopolitics: three vessels were struck in/near Hormuz within 24 hours (laden Qatari LNG carrier Al Rekayyat, Saudi VLCC Wedyan, plus a third ship); the US re-imposed Iran oil sanctions and CENTCOM struck Iranian sites near the Strait. Oil (+1.3%) and EU gas (+8.2%) jumped — war-risk premia on metal, freight and insurance are re-bidding after weeks of unwind.

China: destocking is accelerating — warehouse withdrawals hit a four-year high, billet inventory fell to ~130kt (a near-three-year low) and A00 spot firmed to ¥22,940 (+¥100); SHFE closed the night session at ¥23,055 (+0.5%), still ~$118 under the LME 3M, keeping the import window shut.

Watch this week: FOMC June minutes today (10Y at 4.50%, a two-week high; Sep hike odds ~58%); China June CPI/PPI Thursday; whether MJP spot ($380, SMM) completes convergence to the $395 Q3 settlement; and Alcoa's Q2 on 16-Jul opening the sector's earnings season.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
